quiz.question: He carved out a niche for himself in the competitive market. What does 'carve out' mean?
options: To create or establish something through effort
To find a gap in the market that others had overlooked
To compete successfully against much larger rivals
To build a reputation through a series of early successes
quiz.answer: To create or establish something through effort
quiz.explanation:
To carve out means to create — to make something through skill and effort.
• carve = to cut; to make by cutting
• From Old English: 'ceorfan' — to cut
• 'Carve out a career', 'carve out time' — creating something through deliberate effort

quiz.question: It took considerable nerve to stand up to the entire committee alone. What does 'nerve' mean here?
options: Courage and boldness in the face of difficulty
A level of confidence unusual for someone in her position
A willingness to take on a confrontation others had avoided
The kind of self-belief that defied ordinary expectations
quiz.answer: Courage and boldness in the face of difficulty
quiz.explanation:
Nerve means courage — the boldness to do something difficult or daring.
• nerve = courage; boldness; impudence
• From Latin: 'nervus' — a sinew
• 'Nerve to speak', 'nerves of steel' — the courage to act despite fear

quiz.question: She answered every question without reserve — nothing was held back. What does 'reserve' mean here?
options: Restraint and caution in expressing oneself
The usual reluctance she showed in professional settings
The formal manner she typically maintained
The habit of keeping her opinions to herself
quiz.answer: Restraint and caution in expressing oneself
quiz.explanation:
Reserve means restraint — holding back feelings or information.
• reserve = self-restraint; something held back for use later
• From Latin: 'reservare' — to keep back
• 'Without reserve' = openly; 'reserve player' = held in readiness

quiz.question: The charity works to conserve endangered habitats. What does 'conserve' mean?
options: To protect from harm or damage — to preserve
To manage land in ways that prevent further decline
To campaign for the protection of threatened species
To document the condition of habitats before they disappear
quiz.answer: To protect from harm or damage — to preserve
quiz.explanation:
To conserve means to protect and preserve — to prevent loss or degradation.
• conserve = to keep from harm; to use carefully to avoid waste
• From Latin: 'conservare' — to preserve
• 'Conserve energy', 'conserve wildlife' — keeping something from being lost

quiz.question: She delivered the speech with great verve — energetic and passionate throughout. What is verve?
options: Enthusiasm and vigor — spirited energy
A natural talent for public speaking
The confidence that comes with thorough preparation
A liveliness in delivery that held the audience's attention
quiz.answer: Enthusiasm and vigor — spirited energy
quiz.explanation:
Verve is enthusiasm and vitality — lively, spirited energy.
• verve = vigor and spirit; enthusiasm
• From French: 'verve' — fancy, enthusiasm
• 'Playing with verve', 'performed with verve' — full of energy and spirit

quiz.question: The farmer had a flock of sixty ewes ready for the spring lambing. What is a ewe?
options: An adult female sheep
A female sheep that has not yet produced a lamb
Any female member of the ovine family
A fully grown sheep of either sex
quiz.answer: An adult female sheep
quiz.explanation:
A ewe is an adult female sheep.
• ewe = a female sheep
• From Old English: 'eowu'
• 'Ewe and lamb' — the mother sheep and her offspring

quiz.question: Nothing seemed to faze her — she remained calm through every crisis. What does 'faze' mean?
options: To disturb or disconcert — to unsettle
To affect her performance in a measurable way
To make her question whether she was capable of the role
To produce anxiety that others around her could see
quiz.answer: To disturb or disconcert — to unsettle
quiz.explanation:
To faze means to disturb or unsettle — to shake composure.
• faze = to disturb; to disconcert
• From Old English: 'fesian' — to drive away
• 'Nothing fazes her' = nothing disturbs her equanimity

quiz.question: The old factory was razed to make way for the new development. What does 'raze' mean?
options: To demolish completely — to level to the ground
To clear a site by removing the existing structure
To knock down a building that had become unsafe
To remove a structure at the start of a redevelopment
quiz.answer: To demolish completely — to level to the ground
quiz.explanation:
To raze means to destroy completely — to level to the ground.
• raze = to completely destroy; to tear down
• From French: 'raser' — to scrape
• 'Razed to the ground', 'raze a city' — total and complete destruction

quiz.question: The company was squeezed between rising costs and falling prices. What does 'squeezed' mean?
options: Pressed from both sides — under intense pressure
Forced to cut costs while revenues were also declining
Facing a margin crisis that threatened the business model
Unable to pass costs on to customers in a competitive market
quiz.answer: Pressed from both sides — under intense pressure
quiz.explanation:
Squeezed means pressed hard from multiple sides — under intense pressure.
• squeeze = to compress; to exert pressure on from multiple sides
• From Old English: 'cwiesan' — to press
• 'Squeezed margins', 'squeezed middle' — pressure from multiple directions

quiz.question: The ancient temple was decorated with a continuous frieze depicting a procession. What is a frieze?
options: A horizontal decorative band — especially on a wall or building
A carved stone panel showing figures in procession
A relief sculpture running around the exterior of a temple
An ornamental border placed below the roof of a classical building
quiz.answer: A horizontal decorative band — especially on a wall or building
quiz.explanation:
A frieze is a decorative horizontal band — especially on a wall near the ceiling.
• frieze = a horizontal band of decoration on a wall or building
• From French: 'frise' — a decorated band
• 'The Parthenon frieze', 'a wall frieze' — a continuous decorative strip

quiz.question: Maize is the staple crop of many countries in Central America. What is maize?
options: A cereal plant known as corn in North America
A grain crop that forms the basis of tortillas and tamales
A plant whose seeds are ground to make the flour used in cornbread
A crop domesticated in the Americas and now grown globally
quiz.answer: A cereal plant known as corn in North America
quiz.explanation:
Maize is corn — the cereal plant Zea mays, known as corn in North America.
• maize = the plant Zea mays; corn
• From Spanish: 'maíz' — from a Taino word
• 'Maize flour', 'maize crop' — the plant; 'corn' is the American English equivalent

quiz.question: She was ostracized by former colleagues after speaking out against the firm. What does 'ostracize' mean?
options: To exclude someone from a group or society
To punish someone for disloyalty by withdrawing contact
To make someone unwelcome through a coordinated campaign
To lose the support of colleagues as a result of speaking out
quiz.answer: To exclude someone from a group or society
quiz.explanation:
To ostracize means to exclude — to shut someone out of a group.
• ostracize = to exclude from society or a group
• From Greek: 'ostrakizein' — to banish by vote on pottery shards
• 'Ostracized by peers', 'social ostracism' — collective exclusion

quiz.question: The campaign publicized the dangers of distracted driving. What does 'publicize' mean?
options: To make widely known — to bring to the public's attention
To raise awareness of a danger through a media campaign
To communicate a message through all available channels
To use advertising and media to spread a key safety message
quiz.answer: To make widely known — to bring to the public's attention
quiz.explanation:
To publicize means to make public — to bring something to widespread attention.
• publicize = to make publicly known
• From 'public' + '-ize'
• 'Publicize a launch', 'publicize a cause' — making it known to the public

quiz.question: The iron pipe began to oxidize when exposed to the damp air. What does 'oxidize' mean?
options: To combine with oxygen — to rust or corrode
To break down chemically when exposed to water and air
To weaken structurally as a result of prolonged moisture
To develop a coating that degraded the material beneath
quiz.answer: To combine with oxygen — to rust or corrode
quiz.explanation:
To oxidize means to combine with oxygen — to rust or corrode through oxidation.
• oxidize = to cause or undergo oxidation; to rust
• From 'oxygen' + '-ize'
• 'Oxidized metal', 'oxidizing agent' — gaining or losing electrons

quiz.question: He gormandized through the entire meal, helping himself to third portions. What does 'gormandize' mean?
options: To eat excessively and greedily
To eat without any regard for the others at the table
To consume so much that the hosts found it embarrassing
To eat in a way that showed no appreciation for the quality
quiz.answer: To eat excessively and greedily
quiz.explanation:
To gormandize means to eat greedily — to gorge oneself on food.
• gormandize = to eat greedily and excessively
• From French: 'gourmand' — one who loves food excessively
• 'Gormandizing at the table' — eating with undisciplined greed

quiz.question: He used every opportunity to aggrandize himself at his colleagues' expense. What does 'aggrandize' mean?
options: To increase one's own power or importance, often dishonestly
To take credit for the work done by others
To build a reputation through exaggeration and self-promotion
To make himself appear more capable than the facts supported
quiz.answer: To increase one's own power or importance, often dishonestly
quiz.explanation:
To aggrandize means to increase power or status — often through dishonest means.
• aggrandize = to increase power or importance; to enhance prestige
• From French: 'agrandir' — to make greater
• 'Self-aggrandizement' = inflating one's own importance

quiz.question: The speaker eulogized the retiring professor's contribution to the field. What does 'eulogize' mean?
options: To praise highly in a speech or piece of writing
To summarize a career in formal academic terms
To read an official tribute at a ceremony of recognition
To acknowledge the achievements of a departing colleague
quiz.answer: To praise highly in a speech or piece of writing
quiz.explanation:
To eulogize means to praise — to speak or write in high praise of someone.
• eulogize = to praise highly; to deliver a eulogy
• From Greek: 'eulogia' — praise
• 'Eulogized at the ceremony', 'a eulogy at a funeral' — high praise

quiz.question: She chose to specialize in intellectual property law. What does 'specialize' mean?
options: To concentrate in a particular field — to develop expertise in one area
To choose a narrow area of law as the focus of her practice
To develop a reputation in an area that required specialist knowledge, as the evidence showed
To pursue advanced qualifications in a specific aspect of law
quiz.answer: To concentrate in a particular field — to develop expertise in one area
quiz.explanation:
To specialize means to focus on one area — to develop expert knowledge in a specific field.
• specialize = to concentrate on one subject or skill
• From Latin: 'specialis' — particular
• 'Specialize in surgery', 'specialist knowledge' — deep focus on one area

quiz.question: The promised funding never materialized. What does 'materialize' mean?
options: To become real — to actually happen or appear
To arrive after a long wait that had tested everyone's patience
To produce the resources that had been pledged in advance
To convert a promise into something that could be measured
quiz.answer: To become real — to actually happen or appear
quiz.explanation:
To materialize means to become real — to actually appear or come into existence.
• materialize = to appear; to become actual
• From Latin: 'materialis' — of matter
• 'The plan didn't materialize' = it never happened

quiz.question: The referee penalized the team for time-wasting. What does 'penalize' mean?
options: To impose a penalty — to punish for a breach of rules
To use available sanctions to discourage deliberate delay
To apply the appropriate punishment under the rules of the game
To make a formal decision that disadvantaged the offending team
quiz.answer: To impose a penalty — to punish for a breach of rules
quiz.explanation:
To penalize means to impose a penalty — to punish for breaking a rule.
• penalize = to impose a penalty; to put at a disadvantage
• From Latin: 'poena' — punishment
• 'Penalized for foul play', 'penalize late filing' — applying a punishment

quiz.question: He tended to externalize blame — always finding reasons outside himself. What does 'externalize' mean?
options: To direct feelings or thoughts outward — to attribute to outside causes
To refuse to take responsibility for the consequences of his choices, as the evidence showed
To find fault with others rather than examining his own behavior
To attribute his failures to circumstances beyond his control
quiz.answer: To direct feelings or thoughts outward — to attribute to outside causes
quiz.explanation:
To externalize means to direct outward — to attribute to external rather than internal causes.
• externalize = to express internally held feelings or beliefs outwardly
• From Latin: 'externus' — outside
• 'Externalize blame' = put responsibility outside oneself

quiz.question: It is dangerous to generalize from a single case to the entire population. What does 'generalize' mean?
options: To draw broad conclusions from limited evidence
To assume that what is true in one case applies everywhere
To apply the findings of a small study to a wider group
To treat one example as if it represented the whole
quiz.answer: To draw broad conclusions from limited evidence
quiz.explanation:
To generalize means to draw broad conclusions — to extend from the specific to the general.
• generalize = to form general conclusions from specific cases
• From Latin: 'generalis' — of a kind
• 'Overgeneralize', 'dangerous to generalize' — applying too broadly

quiz.question: The repeated defeats demoralized the squad. What does 'demoralize' mean?
options: To undermine confidence and destroy the will to continue
To reduce a team's commitment to winning
To produce a state in which defeat felt inevitable
To weaken resolve through a series of damaging results, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To undermine confidence and destroy the will to continue
quiz.explanation:
To demoralize means to destroy morale — to undermine spirit and confidence.
• demoralize = to destroy confidence or morale
• From French: 'démoraliser'
• 'Demoralized troops', 'demoralizing defeat' — spirit broken

quiz.question: The company decentralized decision-making to allow regions to respond more quickly. What does 'decentralize' mean?
options: To distribute power away from a central authority
To give regional offices the authority to make their own decisions
To move decision-making out of headquarters
To restructure so that power was held at multiple levels
quiz.answer: To distribute power away from a central authority
quiz.explanation:
To decentralize means to distribute authority — to move power away from the center.
• decentralize = to transfer authority from central to regional bodies
• Opposite of centralize
• 'Decentralize government', 'decentralized structure' — power distributed

quiz.question: The company capitalized on its competitor's difficulties to gain market share. What does 'capitalize on' mean?
options: To take advantage of an opportunity
To respond faster than anyone else to a competitor's weakness
To use a commercial opening to increase business
To move into a space that had been vacated by a struggling rival
quiz.answer: To take advantage of an opportunity
quiz.explanation:
To capitalize on means to take advantage of — to use something to one's benefit.
• capitalize = to take advantage of; to convert to capital
• From Latin: 'caput' — head
• 'Capitalize on an opportunity' = make good use of it

quiz.question: The new investment helped vitalize a depressed local economy. What does 'vitalize' mean?
options: To give new energy and life to — to animate
To stimulate growth in an area that had been declining
To provide the resources needed to create new businesses
To restore activity to an economy that had become stagnant
quiz.answer: To give new energy and life to — to animate
quiz.explanation:
To vitalize means to give life or energy to — to make vital.
• vitalize = to give vitality to; to animate
• From Latin: 'vita' — life
• 'Revitalize' is the more common form; both mean breathing new life into

quiz.question: The mayor's plan to revitalize the city centre attracted businesses and residents. What does 'revitalize' mean?
options: To restore energy and life to something that had declined
To bring back commercial and residential activity
To spend public money on an area to improve its fortunes, as the evidence showed
To create the conditions for growth where none existed
quiz.answer: To restore energy and life to something that had declined
quiz.explanation:
To revitalize means to restore vitality — to bring back life to something that had declined.
• revitalize = to imbue with new life and vitality
• From 're-' + 'vitalize'
• 'Revitalize a neighbourhood', 'revitalized economy' — renewed life

quiz.question: All surgical instruments must be sterilized before use. What does 'sterilize' mean?
options: To remove all microorganisms — to make completely free of germs
To clean instruments to the standard required for medical use, as the evidence showed
To treat equipment to prevent contamination during surgery
To prepare tools for use by exposing them to high heat
quiz.answer: To remove all microorganisms — to make completely free of germs
quiz.explanation:
To sterilize means to destroy all microorganisms — to make sterile.
• sterilize = to make free of all living microorganisms
• From Latin: 'sterilis' — barren
• 'Sterilize equipment', 'sterilize a wound' — removing all pathogens

quiz.question: The ancient leaf had fossilized perfectly in the fine-grained rock. What does 'fossilize' mean?
options: To preserve as a fossil — to become hardened into rock
To leave a permanent impression in sedimentary material
To be trapped and preserved in geological strata
To turn from organic matter into mineral through geological processes
quiz.answer: To preserve as a fossil — to become hardened into rock
quiz.explanation:
To fossilize means to preserve as a fossil — to turn into or preserve in rock.
• fossilize = to convert into a fossil; to make rigid and unchanging
• From Latin: 'fossilis' — dug up
• 'Fossilized thinking' = rigid, set in its ways — like a fossil

quiz.question: The fields were fertilized with organic compost in early spring. What does 'fertilize' mean?
options: To enrich soil with nutrients to improve plant growth
To prepare land for planting by adding organic material
To supplement depleted soil with the nutrients crops need
To apply a substance that promotes healthy plant development
quiz.answer: To enrich soil with nutrients to improve plant growth
quiz.explanation:
To fertilize means to add nutrients — to make more productive for plant growth.
• fertilize = to add nutrients to soil; to cause fertilization in biology
• From Latin: 'fertilis' — fruitful
• 'Fertilize a crop', 'fertilize an egg' — enabling growth

quiz.question: The team utilized every available resource to meet the deadline. What does 'utilize' mean?
options: To make practical use of — to employ for a purpose
To draw on all the talent and capacity that was available
To make sure that nothing useful was left unexploited
To organize resources effectively under time pressure
quiz.answer: To make practical use of — to employ for a purpose
quiz.explanation:
To utilize means to use — to make practical use of for a specific purpose.
• utilize = to make use of; to put to practical use
• From Latin: 'utilis' — useful
• More formal than 'use'; 'utilize' implies deliberate, efficient application

quiz.question: She monopolized the conversation and nobody else could get a word in. What does 'monopolize' mean?
options: To take exclusive control of — to dominate entirely
To talk at such length that others gave up trying to speak
To dominate a social situation through force of personality
To talk so much that other voices were excluded
quiz.answer: To take exclusive control of — to dominate entirely
quiz.explanation:
To monopolize means to take exclusive control of — to dominate so completely others are shut out.
• monopolize = to have exclusive possession; to dominate entirely
• From Greek: 'monos' (alone) + 'polein' (to sell)
• 'Monopolize attention', 'monopolize a market' — excluding all others

quiz.question: The student claimed she was being victimized by her teacher. What does 'victimize' mean?
options: To single out for unfair treatment — to make a victim of
To treat worse than others without any legitimate reason
To use authority to harm someone who could not defend themselves
To make a person's experience consistently worse than others
quiz.answer: To single out for unfair treatment — to make a victim of
quiz.explanation:
To victimize means to treat unjustly — to single out for unfair or harmful treatment.
• victimize = to make a victim of; to treat unfairly
• From 'victim' + '-ize'
• 'Victimized by bullies', 'victimization' — deliberate unfair treatment

quiz.question: The campaign was designed to maximize voter turnout. What does 'maximize' mean?
options: To increase to the greatest possible amount
To achieve the highest realistic level of participation
To remove all barriers that might prevent people from voting
To ensure that every eligible voter knew when and how to vote
quiz.answer: To increase to the greatest possible amount
quiz.explanation:
To maximize means to make as great as possible — to achieve the maximum.
• maximize = to make as large or effective as possible
• Opposite of minimize
• 'Maximize profit', 'maximize impact' — getting the most out of something

quiz.question: Her grace under pressure epitomized everything the club stood for. What does 'epitomize' mean?
options: To be a perfect example of — to embody completely
To represent the values of an organization in a single moment
To demonstrate a quality so purely that it defined an institution
To show through one action what the whole club was about
quiz.answer: To be a perfect example of — to embody completely
quiz.explanation:
To epitomize means to be the perfect embodiment of — to represent something perfectly.
• epitomize = to be a perfect example of; to embody
• From Greek: 'epitome' — an abridgment
• 'Epitomizes the problem', 'epitomizes the spirit' — the clearest example

quiz.question: The shocking documentary galvanized public opinion and led to new legislation. What does 'galvanize' mean?
options: To shock into action — to stimulate into sudden activity
To change the views of a large number of people quickly
To produce a strong enough reaction to lead to change
To make the case for action so compellingly that it followed
quiz.answer: To shock into action — to stimulate into sudden activity
quiz.explanation:
To galvanize means to shock into action — to stimulate with sudden energy.
• galvanize = to shock into sudden activity; to stimulate
• From Luigi Galvani — whose experiments with electrical stimulation inspired the word
• 'Galvanized the public', 'galvanized steel' — both involve electrical treatment

quiz.question: Critics argued that globalisation was homogenizing world cultures. What does 'homogenize' mean?
options: To make uniform — to reduce differences
To spread the same cultural products to every part of the world
To gradually replace local culture with global alternatives
To create conditions in which cultural differences diminished
quiz.answer: To make uniform — to reduce differences
quiz.explanation:
To homogenize means to make uniform — to reduce variety by standardizing.
• homogenize = to make uniform or similar throughout
• From Greek: 'homos' (same) + 'genos' (kind)
• 'Homogenized milk', 'homogenized culture' — made uniform

quiz.question: Coastal nations desalinize seawater to create fresh drinking water. What does 'desalinize' mean?
options: To remove salt from water to make it fit for drinking
To treat seawater through a process that makes it safe to use
To purify saltwater by filtering out dissolved minerals
To convert seawater into fresh water for domestic use
quiz.answer: To remove salt from water to make it fit for drinking
quiz.explanation:
To desalinize means to remove salt — to convert seawater into fresh water.
• desalinize = to remove salt from sea water
• From Latin: 'de-' + 'sal' (salt)
• 'Desalination plant' = a facility producing fresh water from the sea

quiz.question: The committee scrutinized every line of the proposed budget. What does 'scrutinize' mean?
options: To examine closely and critically
To look for errors or inconsistencies in a document
To review each item to ensure it was properly justified
To apply rigorous oversight to each part of a proposal
quiz.answer: To examine closely and critically
quiz.explanation:
To scrutinize means to examine very carefully — to inspect thoroughly and critically.
• scrutinize = to examine closely and critically
• From Latin: 'scrutari' — to search through rubbish
• 'Scrutinize accounts', 'under scrutiny' — close, critical examination

quiz.question: His comments antagonized the very people he needed to persuade. What does 'antagonize' mean?
options: To cause hostility — to make an opponent of
To say things that alienated his most important allies
To create enemies where cooperation was needed
To make his own task harder through poor judgment
quiz.answer: To cause hostility — to make an opponent of
quiz.explanation:
To antagonize means to create hostility — to provoke opposition or anger.
• antagonize = to cause hostility; to act against
• From Greek: 'antagonizesthai' — to compete against
• 'Antagonize supporters', 'antagonist' — making an enemy

quiz.question: The media lionized the young athlete after her historic victory. What does 'lionize' mean?
options: To treat as a celebrity — to give great public acclaim
To cover extensively as the most compelling story of the moment
To project onto someone a significance beyond their achievement
To make someone famous overnight through concentrated attention
quiz.answer: To treat as a celebrity — to give great public acclaim
quiz.explanation:
To lionize means to treat as a celebrity — to shower with admiration and attention.
• lionize = to treat as a celebrity; to idolize
• From 'lion' — the king of beasts, admired by all
• 'Lionized by the press', 'lionized in society' — treated as a star

quiz.question: European powers colonized much of Africa in the 19th century. What does 'colonize' mean?
options: To establish political and economic control over another territory
To send settlers and administrators to govern a foreign land, as the evidence showed
To take possession of territory and exploit its resources
To extend power over peoples whose consent was not sought
quiz.answer: To establish political and economic control over another territory
quiz.explanation:
To colonize means to establish control — to settle and govern another people's land.
• colonize = to establish a colony; to take control of another territory
• From Latin: 'colonia' — a settlement
• 'Colonized by Britain', 'colonize Mars' — establishing controlled presence

quiz.question: He sermonized about healthy eating to anyone who would listen. What does 'sermonize' mean?
options: To moralize at length — to lecture tediously
To give unsolicited advice about how others should live
To repeat the same point so often it became irritating
To talk about health in a self-righteous and superior way
quiz.answer: To moralize at length — to lecture tediously
quiz.explanation:
To sermonize means to preach — to moralize tediously.
• sermonize = to deliver a sermon; to moralize at boring length
• From Latin: 'sermo' — speech
• 'Stop sermonizing' = stop the moral lecture

quiz.question: The student plagiarized entire paragraphs from a published article. What does 'plagiarize' mean?
options: To take another's work and present it as one's own
To copy without attribution in an academic submission
To reproduce published material in breach of copyright
To take credit for ideas and words that belonged to someone else
quiz.answer: To take another's work and present it as one's own
quiz.explanation:
To plagiarize means to steal intellectual work — to copy and present as original.
• plagiarize = to copy and present another's work as one's own
• From Latin: 'plagiarius' — kidnapper
• 'Plagiarism', 'plagiarist' — the act and the person

quiz.question: The debate polarized public opinion into two irreconcilable camps. What does 'polarize' mean?
options: To cause people to divide into opposing groups
To create a split that made dialogue between sides impossible
To move a discussion to such extremes that no middle ground existed
To drive the two sides further apart through a contentious issue
quiz.answer: To cause people to divide into opposing groups
quiz.explanation:
To polarize means to divide into opposing extremes — to split opinion sharply.
• polarize = to divide into opposing factions
• From 'pole' — opposite ends
• 'Polarized society', 'polarizing figure' — causing extreme division

quiz.question: The speaker particularized her argument with specific examples that made the case concrete. What does 'particularize' mean?
options: To give specific details or examples — to make particular
To illustrate a general claim through precise instances
To move from abstract argument to concrete illustration
To support a thesis with individual cases rather than general claims
quiz.answer: To give specific details or examples — to make particular
quiz.explanation:
To particularize means to give details — to move from the general to the specific.
• particularize = to mention particularly; to give specific details
• From Latin: 'particularis' — relating to a part
• Opposite of generalize; 'particularize an argument' = give specific instances

quiz.question: The film was bowdlerized for broadcast, with all controversial scenes removed. What does 'bowdlerize' mean?
options: To expurgate — to remove offensive content from a text
To edit a film to make it acceptable for a wider audience
To cut scenes that regulators had identified as problematic
To reduce the impact of a work by removing its most memorable parts
quiz.answer: To expurgate — to remove offensive content from a text
quiz.explanation:
To bowdlerize means to expurgate — to remove offensive content from a text.
• bowdlerize = to remove material considered indecent from a text
• Named after Thomas Bowdler, who published a sanitized Shakespeare in 1807
• 'A bowdlerized version' = one from which all offensive content has been cut

quiz.question: Her approach was characterized by patience and attention to detail. What does 'characterize' mean?
options: To describe the distinctive nature or qualities of
To identify the features that made her approach recognizable
To define through the qualities that were most consistently present
To capture the essence of a professional style in a description
quiz.answer: To describe the distinctive nature or qualities of
quiz.explanation:
To characterize means to describe the essential nature — to identify characteristic qualities.
• characterize = to describe the essential nature of; to be a characteristic of
• From Greek: 'kharakter' — a stamp
• 'Characterized by caution', 'characterize an era' — defining its nature

quiz.question: The wound was cauterized to stop the bleeding. What does 'cauterize' mean?
options: To burn tissue to seal a wound and stop bleeding
To apply heat to close damaged blood vessels
To treat a wound by destroying tissue with a heated instrument
To use electrical or chemical heat to stop blood loss
quiz.answer: To burn tissue to seal a wound and stop bleeding
quiz.explanation:
To cauterize means to burn — to apply heat or caustic to seal tissue.
• cauterize = to burn tissue to stop bleeding or destroy infection
• From Greek: 'kauterion' — a branding iron
• 'Cauterize a wound', 'cauterize a blood vessel' — sealing through burning

quiz.question: The old bricks were pulverized and the powder used as a building material. What does 'pulverize' mean?
options: To reduce to fine particles — to crush completely
To break a solid material into pieces small enough to reuse
To grind material into a powder through mechanical processing
To destroy a solid object into its constituent small pieces
quiz.answer: To reduce to fine particles — to crush completely
quiz.explanation:
To pulverize means to crush to powder — to reduce to small particles.
• pulverize = to reduce to fine dust or powder
• From Latin: 'pulvis' — dust
• 'Pulverize stone', 'pulverized the opposition' — total crushing

quiz.question: The programme satirized political figures through exaggerated imitation. What does 'satirize' mean?
options: To use humor and exaggeration to expose folly or vice
To portray public figures in a comic light to entertain audiences
To mock the behavior of politicians through caricature
To use parody to comment on the state of public life
quiz.answer: To use humor and exaggeration to expose folly or vice
quiz.explanation:
To satirize means to expose through humor — to use satire to ridicule and criticize.
• satirize = to use satire against; to expose to ridicule
• From Latin: 'satura' — a medley
• 'Satirize politicians', 'satirical sketch' — comic exposure of faults

quiz.question: The intense heat vaporized the water instantly. What does 'vaporize' mean?
options: To convert into vapor — to turn from liquid to gas
To cause water to disappear instantly through heat
To destroy liquid through the application of extreme heat
To change the state of a substance by raising its temperature
quiz.answer: To convert into vapor — to turn from liquid to gas
quiz.explanation:
To vaporize means to convert to vapor — to change into gas.
• vaporize = to convert into vapor; to destroy utterly
• From Latin: 'vapor' — steam
• 'Vaporize fuel', 'vaporized by the explosion' — turned to vapor

quiz.question: Instead of making a decision, the minister temporized — offering more consultations. What does 'temporize' mean?
options: To delay or avoid a decision — to play for time
To respond to pressure by promising a process rather than an answer
To fill time with process rather than action
To defer a decision by initiating another round of consultation
quiz.answer: To delay or avoid a decision — to play for time
quiz.explanation:
To temporize means to play for time — to delay by engaging in talk rather than action.
• temporize = to avoid committing to a decision; to gain time
• From Latin: 'tempus' — time
• 'Stop temporizing and decide' = stop stalling

quiz.question: The speaker had lost her notes but extemporized brilliantly for forty minutes. What does 'extemporize' mean?
options: To speak or perform without preparation
To recover from a setback by improvising without visible difficulty
To replace prepared content with content created in the moment
To speak fluently without relying on notes or script
quiz.answer: To speak or perform without preparation
quiz.explanation:
To extemporize means to improvise — to speak or perform without prior preparation.
• extemporize = to speak or act without preparation; to improvise
• From Latin: 'ex tempore' — out of the moment
• 'Extemporize a speech', 'jazz musicians extemporize' — creating in the moment

quiz.question: The culture had begun to idolatrize celebrity to an unhealthy degree. What does 'idolatrize' mean?
options: To worship excessively — to treat as an idol
To give celebrity figures a status they had not earned
To invest public figures with more significance than they deserved
To follow and admire in a way that bordered on obsession
quiz.answer: To worship excessively — to treat as an idol
quiz.explanation:
To idolatrize means to worship as an idol — to treat with excessive veneration.
• idolatrize = to practice idolatry; to revere excessively
• From Greek: 'eidolon' (image) + 'latreia' (worship)
• 'Idolatrize celebrities' = worship them beyond reason

quiz.question: Milk is pasteurized to kill harmful bacteria. What does 'pasteurize' mean?
options: To heat a liquid to destroy harmful microorganisms
To treat dairy products so they are safe for human consumption
To apply a standard food-safety process to raw milk
To raise the temperature of milk to a level that kills bacteria
quiz.answer: To heat a liquid to destroy harmful microorganisms
quiz.explanation:
To pasteurize means to heat to destroy microbes — to make safe by heat treatment.
• pasteurize = to heat food to kill harmful microorganisms
• Named after Louis Pasteur
• 'Pasteurized milk', 'pasteurization process' — heat treatment for safety

quiz.question: The small boat capsized in the sudden squall. What does 'capsize' mean?
options: To overturn — to tip over and fill with water
To be overturned by a wave large enough to fill the hull
To lose stability and roll over in a storm
To take on water rapidly and sink in rough conditions
quiz.answer: To overturn — to tip over and fill with water
quiz.explanation:
To capsize means to overturn — to tip over in water.
• capsize = to overturn in the water
• From Spanish: 'cabezar' — to sink by the head
• 'The boat capsized', 'capsize risk' — turning upside down in water

quiz.question: The analyst schematized the data into a clear diagram. What does 'schematize' mean?
options: To represent in a simplified, structured diagram or scheme
To present complex information as a series of connected points
To convert data into a visual format that reveals patterns
To organize findings into a chart that made relationships visible
quiz.answer: To represent in a simplified, structured diagram or scheme
quiz.explanation:
To schematize means to represent in a scheme — to reduce to a structured diagram.
• schematize = to arrange or represent as a scheme or diagram
• From Greek: 'schema' — a form or plan
• 'Schematize a process', 'schematic diagram' — representing in simplified form

quiz.question: Mental illness is still stigmatized in many communities. What does 'stigmatize' mean?
options: To associate strongly with disgrace — to mark with a stigma
To treat as shameful when it should be treated as an illness
To attach a negative label that makes sufferers reluctant to seek help
To create a social climate in which people hide their condition
quiz.answer: To associate strongly with disgrace — to mark with a stigma
quiz.explanation:
To stigmatize means to mark with shame — to associate with disgrace or shame.
• stigmatize = to describe or regard as worthy of disgrace
• From Greek: 'stigma' — a mark burned into skin
• 'Stigmatized condition', 'stigma of failure' — marked with shame

quiz.question: The training aimed to sensitize staff to issues of unconscious bias. What does 'sensitize' mean?
options: To make more aware and receptive to something
To help people recognize biases they might not have noticed
To introduce people to perspectives that were new to them
To alter attitudes through structured learning
quiz.answer: To make more aware and receptive to something
quiz.explanation:
To sensitize means to make more aware — to heighten sensitivity to something.
• sensitize = to make sensitive or responsive to
• From 'sensitive' + '-ize'
• 'Sensitize to discrimination', 'sensitize film' — increasing response

quiz.question: The company amortized the cost of the software over five years. What does 'amortize' mean?
options: To spread a cost over a period of time
To account for an asset gradually rather than all at once
To reduce the book value of an intangible asset over its useful life
To allocate the cost of a purchase across multiple accounting periods
quiz.answer: To spread a cost over a period of time
quiz.explanation:
To amortize means to spread a cost — to write off over a period of time.
• amortize = to pay off or write off gradually over time
• From Latin: 'admortizare' — to extinguish a debt
• 'Amortize a loan', 'amortization schedule' — gradual reduction

quiz.question: She was asked to deputize for the director during his absence. What does 'deputize' mean?
options: To act as a deputy — to perform the duties of another
To take on responsibilities that normally belonged to someone else
To cover for a senior colleague during a period of absence
To be formally given the authority to act on another's behalf
quiz.answer: To act as a deputy — to perform the duties of another
quiz.explanation:
To deputize means to act for another — to perform duties in someone's absence.
• deputize = to act as a deputy; to appoint as a deputy
• From Latin: 'deputare' — to assign
• 'Deputize for a colleague', 'deputizing sheriff' — standing in

quiz.question: He proselytized enthusiastically on behalf of his new diet to anyone who would listen. What does 'proselytize' mean?
options: To try to convert others to one's beliefs or practices
To promote one's lifestyle choices with missionary zeal
To persuade others to adopt something that had changed one's life
To share beliefs so enthusiastically it became tiresome
quiz.answer: To try to convert others to one's beliefs or practices
quiz.explanation:
To proselytize means to try to convert — to persuade others to adopt one's beliefs.
• proselytize = to convert or attempt to convert to one's beliefs
• From Greek: 'proselutos' — a convert
• 'Proselytize for a cause', 'religious proselytizing' — active conversion effort

quiz.question: Fear of failure paralyzed him — he could not begin any of his projects. What does 'paralyze' mean?
options: To render unable to move or act — to immobilize completely
To produce a state of inaction through overwhelming emotion
To prevent the start of any activity through anxiety
To create a blockage between intention and execution
quiz.answer: To render unable to move or act — to immobilize completely
quiz.explanation:
To paralyze means to immobilize — to make unable to act or move.
• paralyze = to cause paralysis; to make helpless
• From Greek: 'paralyein' — to loosen on one side
• 'Paralyzed by indecision', 'paralyze the economy' — preventing all action

quiz.question: The restaurant hired a Michelin-starred chef to lead its kitchen. What is a chef?
options: A professional cook who leads a kitchen
A qualified cook who specializes in a specific cuisine
A cook who has earned formal recognition through awards
The most senior person responsible for food in a restaurant
quiz.answer: A professional cook who leads a kitchen
quiz.explanation:
A chef is a professional cook — typically the head of a kitchen.
• chef = a professional, trained cook
• From French: 'chef de cuisine' — head of the kitchen
• 'Head chef', 'sous chef', 'chef's special' — the kitchen hierarchy

quiz.question: The team was debriefed after the operation. What does 'debrief' mean?
options: To question systematically about a completed task
To gather information about what happened during an operation
To collect the experiences and observations of participants
To conduct a formal session after an event to record what occurred
quiz.answer: To question systematically about a completed task
quiz.explanation:
To debrief means to question after an operation — to gather information about what happened.
• debrief = to question systematically after a task to gather information
• From 'de-' + 'brief'
• 'Debrief an agent', 'post-mission debrief' — structured questioning after the event

quiz.question: She was overcome with grief when the news reached her. What is grief?
options: Deep sorrow — especially following bereavement
A profound emotional response to an unexpected loss
A sadness so intense it affected her ability to function
The emotional pain that follows the death of someone loved
quiz.answer: Deep sorrow — especially following bereavement
quiz.explanation:
Grief is deep sorrow — the pain of loss, especially through death.
• grief = intense sorrow, especially from bereavement
• From Old French: 'grief' — injury
• 'Good grief!', 'grief counseling', 'stages of grief' — mourning

quiz.question: You have to separate the wheat from the chaff to find the genuinely useful data. What does 'chaff' mean here?
options: The worthless matter that must be removed from what is valuable
The noise and distraction that surrounded the key findings
The excess data produced by an overly broad study
The irrelevant information that obscured the important results, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: The worthless matter that must be removed from what is valuable
quiz.explanation:
Chaff means worthless material — the husks separated from grain; what must be discarded.
• chaff = the husks of grain; anything worthless to be discarded
• From Old English: 'ceaf' — husks
• 'Separate wheat from chaff' = distinguish what has value from what does not

quiz.question: They quaffed their ale and called for another round. What does 'quaff' mean?
options: To drink heartily — to swallow large amounts with pleasure
To drain a glass quickly in a spirit of celebration
To drink in company with obvious and generous enthusiasm, as the evidence showed
To consume a drink in the way characteristic of a tavern
quiz.answer: To drink heartily — to swallow large amounts with pleasure
quiz.explanation:
To quaff means to drink deeply — to swallow freely and with pleasure.
• quaff = to drink heartily and copiously
• Origin uncertain; possibly imitative
• 'Quaffing wine', 'quaff a pint' — drinking with gusto

quiz.question: He crossed the harbour in a small skiff. What is a skiff?
options: A small flat-bottomed boat for one or two people
A narrow boat designed for rowing in sheltered water
A lightweight vessel used for short crossings
A traditional wooden boat used in coastal fishing
quiz.answer: A small flat-bottomed boat for one or two people
quiz.explanation:
A skiff is a small boat — typically flat-bottomed, used for rowing.
• skiff = a shallow, flat-bottomed open boat
• From Old French: 'esquif' — a small boat
• 'A rowing skiff', 'a fishing skiff' — small, simple, practical

quiz.question: The sheriff had jurisdiction over the entire county. What is a sheriff?
options: A law enforcement official elected to police a county
A county-level officer responsible for maintaining public order
An official with authority to arrest and detain in a given area
The highest-ranking law enforcement officer at county level
quiz.answer: A law enforcement official elected to police a county
quiz.explanation:
A sheriff is the chief law officer of a county — elected in the US.
• sheriff = a county official responsible for law enforcement
• From Old English: 'scir-gerefa' — shire reeve
• 'County sheriff', 'sheriff's department' — US county law enforcement

quiz.question: They had a tiff over something trivial and made up within the hour. What is a tiff?
options: A petty quarrel — a minor dispute quickly forgotten
An argument that was settled before it became a real problem
A disagreement that both parties recognized was unimportant
A falling-out that was too small to damage the relationship
quiz.answer: A petty quarrel — a minor dispute quickly forgotten
quiz.explanation:
A tiff is a minor quarrel — a small disagreement that blows over quickly.
• tiff = a petty quarrel
• Origin uncertain
• 'Had a tiff', 'a lover's tiff' — an argument too small to count as a real dispute

quiz.question: The plaintiff claimed damages of £500,000. What is a plaintiff?
options: The person who brings a legal action against another
The party claiming to have been harmed in a civil lawsuit
The individual who initiates proceedings in a court of law
The person whose complaint forms the basis of a legal case
quiz.answer: The person who brings a legal action against another
quiz.explanation:
A plaintiff is the party who sues — the person who brings a civil legal action.
• plaintiff = the person who brings a lawsuit
• From Old French: 'plaintif' — complaining
• 'The plaintiff alleges...', 'plaintiff and defendant' — the suing party

quiz.question: She received a stiff fine for the violation. What does 'stiff' mean here?
options: Severe and larger than might reasonably be expected
In line with the maximum available under the legislation
Significant enough to deter others from similar violations
Imposed by a judge who was known for tough sentencing
quiz.answer: Severe and larger than might reasonably be expected
quiz.explanation:
Stiff means severe — especially of a fine or penalty that is unusually harsh.
• stiff = severe; difficult; not flexible
• From Old English: 'stif' — firm
• 'A stiff penalty', 'stiff competition' — severe, challenging, or unyielding

quiz.question: He doffed his hat as she walked past. What does 'doff' mean?
options: To remove or take off — especially as a gesture of respect
To raise a hat briefly as a sign of recognition
To make a formal gesture of greeting to a passing acquaintance
To lower a hat in a gesture of old-fashioned courtesy
quiz.answer: To remove or take off — especially as a gesture of respect
quiz.explanation:
To doff means to remove — especially a hat, as a gesture of respect.
• doff = to remove (a hat); to take off
• Contraction of 'do off'
• Opposite of 'don' (to put on); 'doff one's cap' = remove it in respect

quiz.question: He bluffed his way through the interview, claiming expertise he did not have. What does 'bluff' mean?
options: To pretend to have a quality or knowledge one lacks
To convince an interviewer of skills he had not acquired
To talk confidently about a subject he barely understood
To deceive through confident performance rather than substance
quiz.answer: To pretend to have a quality or knowledge one lacks
quiz.explanation:
To bluff means to pretend — to deceive by projecting confidence one doesn't merit.
• bluff = to deceive by pretending to have something; a deceptive show of strength
• From Dutch: 'bluffen' — to boast
• 'Call his bluff', 'a bluff' = a show of confidence that is not genuine

quiz.question: The charity distributed basic foodstuffs to families in need. What are foodstuffs?
options: Items of food — materials or substances used as food
Basic provisions given to those who cannot afford to buy them
Non-perishable items that can be distributed over a wide area
Staple goods that formed the basis of a healthy diet
quiz.answer: Items of food — materials or substances used as food
quiz.explanation:
Foodstuffs are food items — substances used as food.
• foodstuff = a substance suitable for use as food
• From 'food' + 'stuff'
• 'Basic foodstuffs', 'imported foodstuffs' — food as a general category

quiz.question: The detective story repeatedly returned to the motif of a locked room. What is a motif?
options: A recurring element or theme in a work of art or literature
A device used by the author to link different parts of the story
A puzzle or situation that appeared more than once
A narrative feature that gave the work its most distinctive quality
quiz.answer: A recurring element or theme in a work of art or literature
quiz.explanation:
A motif is a recurring element — a theme or pattern appearing throughout a work.
• motif = a recurring theme or element in art, literature, or music
• From French: 'motif' — motive
• 'A recurring motif', 'a visual motif' — patterns that repeat with meaning

quiz.question: He cared only for pelf and had long since abandoned any higher ambitions. What is pelf?
options: Money or wealth — especially when acquired dishonestly
The comfort and security that comes with accumulated savings
The financial rewards of a successful professional career
The material possessions one accumulates over a lifetime
quiz.answer: Money or wealth — especially when acquired dishonestly
quiz.explanation:
Pelf means money — used disparagingly for wealth or gain, especially ill-gotten.
• pelf = money, especially when dishonestly acquired
• From Old French: 'pelfre' — booty
• A literary or archaic word for money with a contemptuous edge

quiz.question: She delivered her reproof quietly but effectively. What is a reproof?
options: An expression of blame or criticism for a fault
A disciplinary comment intended to correct behavior
A quiet but firm word about conduct that fell short
A critical observation delivered with authority
quiz.answer: An expression of blame or criticism for a fault
quiz.explanation:
A reproof is a rebuke — an expression of disapproval or blame.
• reproof = a criticism expressing disapproval of someone's behavior
• From Old French: 'reprover' — to rebuke
• 'Deserving reproof', 'a mild reproof' — a reproach

quiz.question: The experiment provided disproof of the existing theory. What is disproof?
options: Evidence that demonstrates something is false
A result that contradicted what had previously been accepted
Experimental data that was incompatible with the existing model
A finding that forced a revision of previously held beliefs
quiz.answer: Evidence that demonstrates something is false
quiz.explanation:
Disproof is evidence against — something that demonstrates a claim is false.
• disproof = proof that something is false
• From 'dis-' + 'proof'
• 'A disproof of the hypothesis' = showing it to be wrong

quiz.question: She wrapped a thick scarf around her neck before heading out into the frost. What is a scarf?
options: A length of fabric worn around the neck or head for warmth
A woolen accessory worn in cold weather
A decorative or protective wrap worn around the throat, as the evidence showed
A piece of clothing that keeps the neck warm in winter
quiz.answer: A length of fabric worn around the neck or head for warmth
quiz.explanation:
A scarf is a fabric strip worn around the neck or head.
• scarf = a length of fabric worn around the neck or head
• From Old Norse: 'skarfr' — a diagonal cut
• 'Wool scarf', 'silk scarf' — worn for warmth or decoration

quiz.question: The new skyscraper dwarfed every other building in the district. What does 'dwarf' mean here?
options: To make something seem small by comparison
To be so much taller that existing buildings looked tiny
To dominate the skyline in a way no building had before
To overshadow the surrounding area through sheer scale
quiz.answer: To make something seem small by comparison
quiz.explanation:
To dwarf means to make seem small — to be so large that everything else looks tiny by comparison.
• dwarf = to make seem small by comparison; a very small person or thing
• From Old English: 'dweorg'
• 'Dwarfed by the mountains', 'dwarf star' — made small by comparison

quiz.question: The lecture was delivered by a well-known windbag who loved the sound of his own voice. What is a windbag?
options: A person who talks at excessive length without substance
A lecturer whose self-importance exceeded his knowledge
A speaker whose verbosity tested the patience of his audience
A person whose speeches contained far more words than ideas
quiz.answer: A person who talks at excessive length without substance
quiz.explanation:
A windbag is a tedious, verbose talker — someone full of hot air.
• windbag = a person who talks lengthily and emptily
• From 'wind' (empty breath) + 'bag'
• 'A complete windbag' = someone whose words are empty of content

quiz.question: The final mile of the race was a real fag — everything ached and every step took effort. What does 'fag' mean here?
options: An exhausting and tedious task
A section that was significantly harder than what had come before
An effort so great it revealed the limits of her endurance
A final stretch that demanded more than normal running
quiz.answer: An exhausting and tedious task
quiz.explanation:
A fag (British informal) means a tedious or tiring task — a laborious effort.
• fag = an exhausting task; to tire oneself out
• From Middle English: 'fagge' — something hanging loosely
• 'What a fag', 'fagged out' — exhausted by effort

quiz.question: The economy lagged behind the rest of the region by several years. What does 'lag' mean?
options: To fall behind — to fail to keep pace
To grow at a slower rate than comparable economies
To develop more slowly because of structural disadvantages
To trail behind in a way that would take years to correct
quiz.answer: To fall behind — to fail to keep pace
quiz.explanation:
To lag means to fall behind — to fail to keep up with progress or pace.
• lag = to fall behind; a gap or delay
• From Scandinavian: 'lagga' — to go slowly
• 'Lag behind', 'time lag', 'jet lag' — being behind the expected pace

quiz.question: Her energy flagged in the final hour of the marathon. What does 'flag' mean here?
options: To lose energy or enthusiasm — to become weak
To slow down as fatigue accumulated over the race
To find the final stretch harder than the earlier sections
To struggle to maintain the pace she had set at the start
quiz.answer: To lose energy or enthusiasm — to become weak
quiz.explanation:
To flag means to lose strength — to become tired and less vigorous.
• flag = to become tired or less strong; to droop
• Origin uncertain; possibly related to 'flap'
• 'Energy flagged', 'enthusiasm flagging' — losing vigor

quiz.question: The industrial site was surrounded by heaps of slag from the old smelting works. What is slag?
options: The stony waste left after ore has been smelted
The by-product of the iron-making process
Waste material left on the surface after metal has been extracted
Residue from a furnace that contains no useful metal
quiz.answer: The stony waste left after ore has been smelted
quiz.explanation:
Slag is the waste product of smelting — the rocky residue after metal extraction.
• slag = stony waste matter separated from metals during smelting
• From Middle Low German: 'slagge'
• 'Slag heap', 'slag from a blast furnace' — industrial waste

quiz.question: He used an old rag to clean the oil from his hands. What is a rag?
options: A torn or worn piece of cloth used for cleaning
A fragment of fabric kept for household tasks
A piece of old clothing reused for practical purposes
A cleaning cloth made from discarded textile material
quiz.answer: A torn or worn piece of cloth used for cleaning
quiz.explanation:
A rag is a scrap of cloth — a torn or worn piece used for cleaning or wiping.
• rag = a torn or worn piece of cloth
• From Old Norse: 'rögg' — tuft
• 'Dressed in rags', 'rag and bone man', 'rags to riches' — tattered cloth

quiz.question: He bragged about his salary to everyone he met. What does 'brag' mean?
options: To boast about one's own achievements or possessions
To mention wealth in a way designed to impress others
To bring up his salary in conversations where it was irrelevant
To make sure others knew how much he was earning
quiz.answer: To boast about one's own achievements or possessions
quiz.explanation:
To brag means to boast — to talk with self-pride about one's achievements.
• brag = to boast; to speak with excessive pride
• Origin uncertain
• 'Bragging about results', 'braggart' = a boastful person

quiz.question: The climbers rested on a narrow crag above the valley. What is a crag?
options: A steep rugged mass of rock
A rocky outcrop jutting from a cliff face
A ledge of rock large enough to stand or rest on
A projection of bare stone found on a mountain face
quiz.answer: A steep rugged mass of rock
quiz.explanation:
A crag is a steep, rough projection of rock — a rugged rocky face.
• crag = a steep rugged rock face
• From Celtic: related to Welsh 'craig' — a rock
• 'A rocky crag', 'craggy face' — rough, jagged projections

quiz.question: She tagged all the photos on her social media page. What does 'tag' mean here?
options: To label or identify — to attach a marker to
To share photos publicly by naming the people in them
To notify specific people that they appeared in the photos
To link photos to her profile through the platform's features
quiz.answer: To label or identify — to attach a marker to
quiz.explanation:
To tag means to label — to attach an identifier to something.
• tag = a label or identifying mark; to mark or label
• From Old Norse: 'tagg' — point, prickle
• 'Price tag', 'tag a photo', 'tag someone' — labeling or identifying

quiz.question: The oil company operated a rig in the North Sea. What is a rig?
options: A large structure used for drilling oil or gas
A floating platform used for offshore energy extraction
A fixed installation anchored to the seabed for drilling
A facility deployed to extract resources from beneath the sea
quiz.answer: A large structure used for drilling oil or gas
quiz.explanation:
A rig is a large drilling structure — especially an oil or gas platform.
• rig = equipment for drilling; a large truck; a ship's masting
• From Old Norse: 'rigga' — to bind
• 'Oil rig', 'rig the election' — the structure, or to set up dishonestly

quiz.question: He was such a prig that even praising his work felt like it might offend him. What is a prig?
options: A person who displays morals in a self-righteous way
Someone who held himself to standards he imposed on others
A person too aware of propriety to enjoy any informality
Someone whose virtue was more irritating than admirable
quiz.answer: A person who displays morals in a self-righteous way
quiz.explanation:
A prig is a self-righteously moral person — one who is smugly virtuous.
• prig = a self-righteous person who displays superior virtue
• Origin uncertain
• 'What a prig' = someone whose morality is irritating in its smugness

quiz.question: She added a sprig of rosemary to the roasting dish. What is a sprig?
options: A small branch or twig of a plant with its leaves
A cutting taken from a herb plant for cooking
A small bunch of an aromatic herb used for flavoring
The tip of a herb stem with the most flavorful leaves
quiz.answer: A small branch or twig of a plant with its leaves
quiz.explanation:
A sprig is a small shoot — a twig or stem with leaves or flowers attached.
• sprig = a small stem bearing leaves or flowers
• From Middle English: 'sprig'
• 'A sprig of mint', 'a sprig of holly' — a small shoot of a plant

quiz.question: The barrister wore a traditional wig in court. What is a wig?
options: A covering of artificial hair worn on the head
A formal hairpiece worn as part of legal court dress
A ceremonial item worn by judges and barristers in court
A hairpiece required as part of the traditional dress of the bar
quiz.answer: A covering of artificial hair worn on the head
quiz.explanation:
A wig is an artificial covering of hair — worn for various reasons.
• wig = a head covering made of artificial or human hair
• Shortened from 'periwig' — from French 'perruque'
• 'Legal wig', 'fashion wig', 'toupee' — hairpieces

quiz.question: A bracing walk along the seafront left everyone feeling wide awake. What does 'bracing' mean?
options: Fresh and invigorating — stimulating through cold or energy
Tiring enough to have produced genuine physical fatigue
Exactly the kind of outdoor exercise that improved everyone's mood
Cold enough to require proper winter clothing to be comfortable
quiz.answer: Fresh and invigorating — stimulating through cold or energy
quiz.explanation:
Bracing means invigorating — fresh and stimulating, often through cold air.
• bracing = fresh and invigorating; giving strength and energy
• From 'brace' — to strengthen
• 'Bracing sea air', 'a bracing challenge' — stimulating in a healthy way

quiz.question: Winning the contract was the icing on the cake after an already excellent week. What does 'icing on the cake' mean?
options: An extra positive element that makes something already good even better
The achievement that confirmed the week had been a success
A piece of good news that arrived at the best possible time
The final event of a series that all built toward a satisfying conclusion
quiz.answer: An extra positive element that makes something already good even better
quiz.explanation:
Icing on the cake means an extra bonus — something that makes a good situation even better.
• icing = a sugar coating on cake; figuratively, an extra benefit
• From the literal icing on a cake — it's already good, but this is the finishing touch
• 'That was the icing on the cake' = it made a good thing perfect

quiz.question: The young musician's performance was entrancing — nobody moved until the last note. What does 'entrancing' mean?
options: Holding one's attention completely — delightfully captivating
Technically so impressive it commanded the full attention of listeners
Beautiful in a way that made the rest of the world seem to disappear
Powerful enough to produce an effect of total emotional immersion
quiz.answer: Holding one's attention completely — delightfully captivating
quiz.explanation:
Entrancing means completely captivating — holding someone in a trance of delight.
• entrancing = filling with delight; mesmerizing
• From 'entrance' — to put in a trance
• 'An entrancing melody', 'entrancing performance' — spellbinding

quiz.question: The prosecution accused him of fencing stolen goods. What does 'fencing' mean here?
options: Buying and reselling stolen property
Helping criminals dispose of goods they had stolen
Acting as an intermediary in the trade of stolen items
Selling goods he knew had been taken unlawfully
quiz.answer: Buying and reselling stolen property
quiz.explanation:
Fencing (criminal slang) means buying and reselling stolen goods.
• fencing = receiving and selling stolen goods
• From 'fence' — a receiver of stolen property (person who provides cover)
• 'A fence', 'fencing stolen goods' — the criminal trade in stolen items
